faq.title: Answers to the most common questions
faq.subtitle: If your question isn't here, send a message and we'll come back with an analysis within a couple of hours.

items.q: How much does a turnkey website cost?
items.a: Landing — from $3,800. Corporate site — from $7,800. SaaS platform with a design system — from $16,500. We send a precise estimate within 24 hours of the brief.
Landing — 4 weeks, multi-page site — 6–7 weeks, SaaS platform — 8–12 weeks. Timelines are fixed in the contract and tied to milestones.

items.q: How do you work on a project?
items.a: In 2-week sprints. You get a dedicated Telegram channel, a Linear board, and access to Figma. Weekly demos and retros.

items.q: Do you hand off and support after launch?
items.a: Yes. We hand over all code, the repository, the design system, and documentation. Support is on a 20+ hour/month retainer or per scope.

items.q: Do you do design only or development only?
items.a: Yes — you can take any block separately: branding, product design, engineering, or support. Most clients go for the full cycle.
All source files and IP transfer to the client after the final payment. It's locked in by contract.

work.title: Selected case studies
work.subtitle: A long-form look at strategy, design and engineering — six recent product launches from positioning to public release.

lumen.title: Relaunched Lumen — a SaaS for product teams
lumen.summary: Brand, design system, marketing site and paid-traffic landings — shipped in 6 weeks.
lumen.context: Lumen had outgrown its early-stage positioning and matured into a tool for product teams, but the site lagged behind. Demo conversion had been flat since fall.
lumen.challenge: Re-frame the offer for a senior audience — Head of Product, Growth, Data — and put the core value front and center: see your product metrics in minutes, not hours.
lumen.approach: We mapped 4 ICP personas and a message map. In parallel we shipped a Figma design system with tokens, prototyped 12 screens, and built a Next.js 14 marketing site with CMS and analytics. Performance kicked off in week 4 — pilot campaigns went live with the launch.
lumen.results: Demo conversion grew 3.2×, average deal +34%, time on page 3:42. Lumen's team can now update content and ship new landings without us.
lumen.retrospective: The big risk — running marketing in parallel with the build — was managed via daily syncs and a shared Linear board. Next time we'll add a 7-day quiet period after launch: paid traffic on day 1 made early metrics noisy.

atlas.title: Design system and site for Atlas, a neobank
atlas.summary: Brand, Next.js front-end, and a dark theme with 3D hero — 8 weeks to public launch.
atlas.context: Atlas launched as a bank for entrepreneurs: open API, instant cards, project-level expense tracking. The release needed a recognizable brand and a site that explains the value in 5 seconds.
atlas.challenge: No visual brand existed — only a name and a working prototype. Tight timeline to launch, complex product integrations, and regulatory limits on copy.
atlas.approach: Brand identity with a dark palette and an acid accent, plus a tone-of-voice doc. The site shipped with a dark theme, a Three.js hero, an interactive pricing module, and an expense calculator.
atlas.results: Daily applications grew 4.2× over the prior landing. Lighthouse 98 / 100 in production. Landing NPS 72. Atlas moved from waitlist to open beta within 30 days.
atlas.retrospective: Dark-by-default worked — but tightened our contrast bar. We re-did half the illustrations after the first QA pass. Going forward, dark products get a dedicated contrast QA pass before release.

north.title: North: an online courses platform from positioning to launch
north.summary: Brand, landing, app, and an autofunnel — from kickoff to first sales in 10 weeks.
north.context: North was entering the crowded online-courses market with an ambition to be 'not just another school'. Positioning, product, marketing, and an autofunnel all had to land together.
north.challenge: Strong product, no recognizable brand. Team of two founders and two experts, aggressive timeline, fixed launch budget.
north.approach: ICP brief, 3 strategic directions, picked 'learning through craft' as the wedge. Brand system, landing, and app shipped in parallel. Email + Telegram autofunnel went live the same day as the site.
north.results: Purchase CR 9.4%, CAC down 41%, 1,800+ active students by month 3. The team now runs content via CMS and ships new cohorts independently.
north.retrospective: Tight timeline forced ruthless scope cuts — blog and community moved to a retainer. Worth it: the launch felt focused, not rushed.

orbit.title: Brand and product for premium car-sharing Orbit
orbit.summary: Identity, mobile app, and an outdoor + media campaign in the capital — 12 weeks end-to-end.
orbit.context: Orbit entered the premium car-sharing segment with a fleet of 200 vehicles. They needed a brand that didn't read 'mass market' and a product that built trust on first touch.
orbit.challenge: Premium in a market where 90% of players say 'cheap and fast'. We had to communicate the 30-second rental experience and reinforce the impression beyond the app.
orbit.approach: Brand with a cosmic tone, an app with ultra-short flows (open, book, drive), and a launch media campaign in Moscow and St Petersburg.
orbit.results: 120K installs in 2 months, D30 retention 38%, brand awareness in the capital +62%.
orbit.retrospective: We underestimated ASO at launch — the app got buried in the store. We brought in an ASO partner on retainer and broke into top-10 in the category in 3 weeks.

journal.title: Notes from the studio
journal.subtitle: How we ship, design, and think — without filler.

process.title: How we ship a SaaS site in six weeks
process.excerpt: Six weeks from kickoff to live, with strategy, brand, and engineering. Not magic — just a clear process.
process.intro: The most common question we get is how we hit 6 weeks where other studios take 4 months. The short answer: discipline and a single team. The long answer is below.

blocks.text: Week 0 · kickoff

Before week 1 we run a short kickoff: a 90-minute working session with the client team, business metrics, a single product owner on the client side, and a frozen ICP. Without this, week 1 turns into an open-ended conversation about taste.
Weeks 1–2 · strategy and architecture
In parallel: market and ICP research, brand voice, information architecture. The designer starts on tokens and grid; the engineer sets up the repo and base CI. Demo every Friday.
Weeks 3–4 · design and content
Screens in Figma, validation against ICP scenarios, copy in pair with the client. We don't wait for everything to be 'approved' — pilot blocks ship to engineering immediately.
Weeks 5–6 · engineering and launch
Animations, forms, CMS, analytics. By week 5 the site is on staging; we run a beta with 20–30 ICP-matching people and gather feedback before public release.
What breaks the process
More than two points of accountability on the client side, demanding 'final sign-off before engineering starts', a scope change in week 5. Any of those, and the timeline slips.

design-tokens.title: Design tokens that scale
design-tokens.excerpt: What design tokens really are, why they save your team on the 5th project, and how we ship them with Figma + Tailwind.
design-tokens.intro: Design tokens are not 'a table of colors and shadows'. They're a contract between design and engineering. When the contract is missing, you get nights of arguing about hex codes.

blocks.text: Levels of tokens

We split tokens into three levels. Primitive — raw values (#5159ff, 16px). Semantic — intent (color.brand, space.lg). Component — local rules (button.primary.bg). Without the hierarchy, tokens collapse into a junk drawer.
Where tokens live

Source of truth: Figma Variables. We export JSON from Figma and transform it through style-dictionary into CSS custom properties. Tailwind picks them up via config — engineers get t-shirt classes like `text-foreground` and never think about hexes.
Dark mode and a11y
Dark mode is not 'invert the values' — it's a separate set of semantic tokens. We check contrast in Figma and run axe-core in CI. AA contrast is a hard requirement, not a 'nice to have'.
Ownership
One human owner for tokens — usually the lead designer. Token changes are PRs that require an engineer review. It takes 10 minutes a week and saves weeks of chaos at scale.

analytics.title: Analytics that actually drive decisions
analytics.excerpt: Why every product site needs its own funnel, the 6 events that cover 80% of insights, and why we don't trust an aggregate conversion rate.
analytics.intro: A site without analytics is a shop where nobody counts customers. Here is how we set up analytics for every new landing page and why we never optimise for a single "overall conversion".

blocks.text: The 6 events we always track
Hero CTA, sticky CTA, contact form, scroll to 80%, case-study click, pricing click. That is enough to see the problem without drowning in data. Anything else we add only when a hypothesis demands it.
Why aggregate conversion lies

An overall conversion rate hides the most important fork: did the user come from an ICP channel or random traffic? We always split the funnel by at least three sources and judge them separately. It saves us from optimising things that were never broken.
Where the events live
GA4 and Plausible for traffic and attribution. PostHog for the product funnel and session replays. UTM tags carry campaign and creative — without them, two months later nothing tells you what worked.
What we do with the numbers
Once every two weeks we review the dashboard with the client: one hypothesis, one metric, one decision. More than two hypotheses on the table is no longer analytics, it's a ritual.

design-review.title: Design review without the pain — a process that doesn't kill velocity
design-review.excerpt: How we set up design review so the team doesn't lose days to revisions and the client still sees clear progress every Friday.
design-review.intro: Every designer knows that a review can cost two hours or two weeks — it depends entirely on the format. We landed on a simple system after four projects, and it now scales to large teams.

blocks.text: One channel, one board
All revisions live in Figma comments tagged P0/P1/P2 by priority. No Slack threads, no spreadsheets. If a revision isn't in Figma, it doesn't exist. That alone removes 80% of context loss.
Sync once a week, async every day
One 30-minute call on Friday, everything else as comments and Loom videos. Designers stop sitting in meetings and start designing.
The two-rounds rule
Two revision rounds max per screen. If we hit a third round, the brief or the goal is broken — we go back up the stack instead of pushing pixels.
Tones instead of taste
Instead of "I don't like it" we ask the client to pick a tone — more premium, more engineered, more warm. That turns taste into a direction we can iterate against.

lumen-retro.title: Lumen retrospective: what 6 weeks taught us
lumen-retro.excerpt: Demo conversion grew 3.2× — but not because the design 'looked nice'. Here's the real reason.
lumen-retro.intro: Eight weeks have passed since Lumen launched — long enough to look at the numbers and pull honest takeaways. Sharing what worked, what didn't, and what we'll change next time.

blocks.text: What worked

The biggest conversion lift didn't come from the homepage, it came from the role-specific landings — separate pages for PM, Growth, and Data. When the ICP sees their context in 5 seconds, the demo form gets filled out 4× more often.
What didn't work the first time

The pricing calculator in the hero. The idea was an interactive ROI; in practice, users got lost by the third slider. We moved the calculator down to the pricing page and replaced the hero with three clean tiers.
Where 80% of the impact is

80% of the lift came from two screens: hero and social proof. If you have five hours of optimization budget — spend them there. Don't go redesign the FAQ if your hero doesn't communicate value in 5 seconds.
What we'll change next time
Previously we placed social proof near the bottom — clients reached the end and trusted us. Now we're moving logos and one strong quote right under the hero. The next case will be the experiment.
